1.pericope_label: Four Rescues: The Hesed of YHWH
1.synthesis: Psalm 107 opens Book V of the Psalter with a comprehensive theology of rescue, organizing human extremity into four representative scenarios: travelers lost in the desert without food or water (vv. 4–9), prisoners in darkness and chains (vv. 10–16), the sick who are brought to the gates of death (vv. 17–22), and sailors caught in a life-threatening storm (vv. 23–32). Each vignette follows the same four-part pattern: peril, cry to YHWH, rescue, and thanksgiving. Each cycle ends with the refrain: “Let them thank the Lord for his steadfast love, for his wondrous works to the children of man!” The psalm concludes with a meditation on YHWH’s sovereignty over rivers and deserts, exalting the humble and humbling the proud (vv. 33–43), and closes with the wisdom call: “Whoever is wise, let him attend to these things; let them consider the steadfast love of the Lord” (v. 43).
1.historical_context: Psalm 107 opens Book V, which scholars have associated with the postexilic period and the ingathering of scattered Israel. The four vignettes may represent four categories of people who returned from exile—those who came through desert regions, those released from captivity, those sick and recovered, and those who traveled by sea. The storm-stilling section (vv. 25–30) provides the closest verbal antecedent to Mark 4:39: when YHWH “made the storm be still” (yaqem se’arah lidemamah, v. 29), the psalmist uses vocabulary that Mark employs for Jesus’s rebuke of the sea. The hydrological reversals of verses 33–40 (rivers to desert, desert to pools) draw on the Exodus and wilderness traditions.
1.literary_structure: The fourfold rescue structure of Psalm 107 is strictly parallel, with each vignette following the identical four-part form (peril → cry → rescue → refrain), creating a cumulative effect: YHWH rescues from every kind of human extremity. The wisdom conclusion (v. 43) integrates all four vignettes into a single interpretive framework: to “attend to these things” (the hesed of YHWH displayed in rescue) is what it means to be wise. The hydrological sovereignty section (vv. 33–42) broadens the frame from individual rescue to cosmic governance: the same YHWH who rescues individuals from desert and storm also governs the patterns of rain and drought that determine the fortunes of nations.
1.theological_application: Psalm 107 establishes that the proper response to private rescue is public thanksgiving. Each vignette does not end with relief but with the call to “thank the Lord” (yodhu) and “tell of his works with songs of joy” (v. 22). The rescue is incomplete until it has been declared in the assembly. Psalm 107 also resists the reduction of divine hesed to a single domain: YHWH is at work in desert wandering, imprisonment, illness, and storm—no human extremity falls outside the scope of his rescue. The wisdom conclusion (v. 43) transforms the psalm into a hermeneutical guide: to be wise is to learn to see hesed in the full range of human experience.
1.christology: The storm-stilling vignette of Psalm 107:25–30 is the direct scriptural background for Mark 4:35–41. When Jesus rebuked the wind and said to the sea “Peace! Be still!” and “the wind ceased and there was a great calm,” the disciples’ question—“Who then is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?” (Mark 4:41)—is answered by Psalm 107: this is YHWH, the one who makes the storm be still and brings sailors to their desired haven (v. 30). The four rescue vignettes of Psalm 107 are enacted in miniature throughout Jesus’s ministry: he heals the sick (vignette 3), releases the imprisoned demoniac (vignette 2), feeds the hungry (vignette 1), and stills the storm (vignette 4).
